registry_meta.title: Seed control-plane artifact registry
registry_meta.status_note: artifact_status is independent from master-plan task status markers

validation_hook_standards.evidence_required: Required fields present for every artifact or artifact type record
Referenced artifact IDs and edge fields resolve
Source-authority references and material grounding are explicit
Terminology, dependencies, and boundaries are consistent with accepted upstream artifacts
Explicit human acceptance for downstream use
Revalidation required after validator or upstream acceptance change
Only used when real fixtures, harnesses, or test contracts exist

stale_rule_catalog.rule: Accepted downstream artifacts connected by hard dependency edges become stale on accepted source-authority change; reference-only links do not propagate by default.
Hard dependencies auto-stale on accepted upstream change; validator changes require revalidation.
Accepted upstream changes trigger stale review by default; deterministic derivations may opt into auto propagation per dependency edge.
Sync and delta artifacts summarize accepted truth and normally require human stale review on upstream change.
Reference-only edges do not propagate stale status by default.

artifact_types.name: Source authority document
artifact_types.purpose: External or mirrored authority document governing platform meaning

artifact_types.name: Master execution plan record
artifact_types.purpose: Working control document for the program

artifact_types.name: Control-plane spec
artifact_types.purpose: Normative spec for registry, rules, or operating artifacts

artifact_types.name: Control-plane dataset
artifact_types.purpose: Machine-readable registry, graph, or control-plane data

artifact_types.name: Canon or interpretation pack
artifact_types.purpose: Contradiction, glossary, or canon interpretation artifact

artifact_types.name: Semantic contract pack
artifact_types.purpose: Shared semantic contracts and state-machine packs

artifact_types.name: Technical architecture pack
artifact_types.purpose: Technical baseline, Platform Gate, topology, or interface pack

artifact_types.name: Release contract pack
artifact_types.purpose: Platform Gate or release-specific contract pack

artifact_types.name: Reusable execution or governance pack
artifact_types.purpose: Governance, proof, protocol, workflow, applet, or handoff pack

artifact_types.name: Surface or repo architecture pack
artifact_types.purpose: Surface contract, repo/package architecture, or documentation-plane artifact

artifact_types.name: Execution packet
artifact_types.purpose: Bounded implementation packet with whitelist and approval rules

artifact_types.name: Delta, sync, or stale-detection pack
artifact_types.purpose: Delta capture, recurring sync, stale rules, or gate re-check artifact

The accepted P3.3 storage/provenance baseline defines the authoritative event and lineage substrate carried by these contracts.
The accepted P3.2 compiler topology defines the frozen packs, replay bases, and compiler events that cross these boundaries.
The accepted P2.1 object model defines the run, branch, review, approval, proof, delta, and writeback refs carried in boundary messages.
The accepted P2.2 pack defines protocol-kernel fields, run-class defaults, and verifier slots that admission and execution messages must preserve.
The accepted P2.3 invariants require typed authority, review, approval, and lane-local writeback messages.
The accepted P2.4 invariants require replay, failure, and validation events to remain inspectable and attached to run truth.
The accepted P2.6 grammar pack informs which views consume monitor, queue, timeline, ledger, compare, and console events without redefining their meaning.
The accepted P1.5 seam map identifies run identity, writeback separation, approval boundaries, and branch/replay semantics as high-blast-radius seams.
The accepted P1.6 scenarios define replay, lane-local approval, commissioning, and live-monitor continuity cases the contract pack must preserve.
The primitive-set authority defines shared-environment APIs, runtime eventing, approval primitives, and adapter layers this spec grounds.
The release doctrine requires chat-native releases to surface approvals, replay, writeback, and live monitoring over shared contracts.
The north-star authority requires commissioned work, proofs, deltas, and governed execution to remain typed across surfaces and runtimes.
The contract baseline must preserve no hidden transcript truth, no separate backend per view, no silent proposal application, and no agent-builder-first drift.
The accepted P1.3 owner-layer map informs which commands and events terminate in engine, shared environment, or projection layers.

notes: P3.4 accepted boundary-contract baseline defining typed APIs, internal IPC, gateway adapter contracts, and shared event-family semantics.
Accepted for downstream use as the Phase 3 API/event baseline feeding Platform Gate, release doctrine, and later repo/package planning.

artifacts.title: Phase 3 Platform Gate spec and exit audit

dependency_refs.notes: P3.5 scope, sequencing, and carry-forward rules come from the master plan.
The Platform Gate pack reuses accepted P0.1 artifact, validation-hook, and stale-rule semantics.
The accepted P1.4 topology canon defines Platform Gate as the mandatory pre-R1 internal stage.
The accepted P1.5 seam map defines SS-10 and the prelaunch substrate obligations that the gate must test.
The accepted P1.6 scenarios and edge cases define the replay, lane-separation, and transcript-truth failures the gate must catch.
The accepted P1.1 contradiction pack fixes Platform Gate as a hard prerequisite and preserves glossary discipline around substrate semantics.
The accepted P3.1 baseline defines the runtime planes, workers, gateways, and shared data/provenance substrate the gate must prove.
The accepted P3.2 compiler topology defines frozen context, replay basis, span traceability, and recovery behavior that the gate must test.
The accepted P3.3 storage/provenance baseline defines the event and lineage substrate the gate must require.
The accepted P3.4 contract baseline defines the typed boundary rules, no-direct-UI-worker-RPC rule, and event families the gate must enforce.
The accepted P2.1 object model defines the runs, packs, branches, reviews, approvals, proofs, deltas, and writeback proposals that gate tests must traverse.
The accepted P2.2 pack defines admission, proof, verifier, and writeback defaults that gate execution must preserve.
The accepted P2.3 invariants define lane-local writeback, approval points, and authority constraints that the gate must prove are real.
The accepted P2.4 invariants define replay, failure, and proof inspectability expectations that gate tests must verify.
The accepted P2.6 grammar pack defines Live Monitor, Ledger, Timeline, Queue, Compare View, and Console as shared projections over the gated substrate.
The primitive-set authority defines the shared-environment and engine primitives that the gate requires.
The release doctrine defines Platform Gate as the mandatory pre-R1 stage and fixes its place in the release order.
The north-star authority requires the commissioned-work substrate that Platform Gate must prove before public release hardening.
The gate must block transcript truth, silent proposal application, second-stack drift, and repo-shape-driven architecture drift.
The accepted P1.3 owner-layer map informs which primitive/package areas belong to engine, shared environment, or projection layers.

notes: P3.5 accepted Platform Gate baseline defining required primitives, forced package/service areas, exit tests, audit checklist, and blocking failure classes.
Accepted for downstream use as the final Phase 3 architecture gate feeding sync review, release contracts, and later repo/package planning.

artifacts.title: Phase 4 release-to-SDK maturity matrix

dependency_refs.notes: P4.1 scope, sequencing, and carry-forward rules come from the master plan.
The release matrix pack reuses accepted P0.1 artifact, validation-hook, and stale-rule semantics.
The accepted P1.4 topology canon defines Platform Gate through R7 as one ordered path that this matrix maps onto package maturity.
The accepted P1.5 seam map identifies Platform Gate, R4, and R7 as bridge seams whose package floors must remain explicit.
The accepted P1.6 scenarios and edge cases show which substrate capabilities each stage must preserve as maturity rises.
The accepted P2.1 object model defines the shared objects whose APIs, review flows, and continuity the matrix must harden.
The accepted P2.2 taxonomy defines run-class, protocol, verifier, and writeback defaults that stage maturity cannot hand-wave away.
The accepted P2.5 release semantics define what each chat-native stage must project over the shared substrate.
The accepted P2.6 grammar pack informs which surfaces become visible when package areas mature at each release stage.
The accepted P2.3 invariants define the governance and lane-separated writeback floors that later releases must preserve.
The accepted P2.4 invariants define proof, replay, and failure expectations that stage maturity must keep explicit.
The accepted P3.1 baseline defines the runtime planes and process families the release matrix maps onto package areas.
The accepted P3.2 compiler topology defines the context and replay substrate that R1-R3 and later stages force to mature.
The accepted P3.3 storage/provenance baseline defines the lineage and audit substrate that the matrix must carry forward by stage.
The accepted P3.4 contract baseline defines the typed API, IPC, gateway, and event seams whose floors rise across releases.
The accepted P3.5 Platform Gate pack defines the forced package areas and pre-R1 substrate floor that this matrix extends release by release.
The primitive-set authority defines the underlying engine and shared-environment capability surface the matrix is hardening.
The release doctrine defines the fixed Platform Gate through R7 order and the stage promises that package maturity must support.
The north-star authority explains why late-stage package floors must become strong enough for R7 to hand into Task Studio without ontology drift.
The matrix must prevent transcript-truth, silent writeback, second-stack, and repo-shape shortcuts from being smuggled into release doctrine.
The accepted P1.1 glossary and contradiction pack informs cross-stage terminology and the fixed Platform Gate prerequisite.
The accepted P1.3 owner-layer map informs which package areas belong to engine, shared environment, or app projection seams.

notes: P4.1 accepted cross-stage matrix mapping Platform Gate and R1-R7 to forced SDK/package maturity floors.
Accepted for downstream use as the Phase 4 release-doctrine baseline feeding the human-only milestone architecture plan, later release packs, and Phase 6 package planning.

artifacts.title: Phase 3 architecture synchronization pass

The accepted P1.3 owner-layer map informs the no-app-private-ontology and no-view-owned-truth checks in this synchronization pass.

notes: P3.6 accepted architecture synchronization pass confirming the Phase 1-4 accepted architecture stack remains aligned and that no accepted Phase 3 artifact is stale at acceptance time.
Accepted for downstream use as the final Phase 3 sync lock feeding the Phase 4 milestone architecture plan, later release contracts, and Phase 6 repo/package planning.

artifacts.title: Phase 4 R1 Transcript Chat contract pack

The R1 contract pack reuses accepted P0.1 artifact, validation-hook, and stale-rule semantics.

The accepted P1.6 scenario corpus defines `GS-01` and `EC-10` as protected R1 pathways and failure cases.

The accepted P2.5 semantic pack defines the R1 shared-object substrate, projection additions, and handoff obligation to R2.

The accepted P3.1 baseline defines the shared control/data/event substrate that R1 must project rather than replace.
The accepted P3.2 compiler topology defines the frozen-context behavior and source-grounded compilation that R1 must surface compactly.
The accepted P3.3 storage/provenance baseline defines the lineage and continuity substrate that keeps R1 threads from collapsing into provider transcript state.
The accepted P3.4 contract baseline defines the control/event boundaries and compact inspection pathways that R1 must preserve.
The accepted P3.5 Platform Gate artifact defines the mandatory pre-R1 blocker and the `PG-01` through `PG-03` substrate proofs R1 inherits.
The accepted P3.6 sync pass confirms the accepted architecture baseline remains aligned before the R1 contract is fixed.
The accepted P4.1 maturity matrix defines the `M3` and `M2` package floors that R1 must treat as binding.
The accepted P4.2 milestone plan defines R1 as the familiar-chat stage with bounded-run continuity and compact inspection, plus the non-goals it must not overbuild past.
The accepted P1.1 glossary and contradiction pack informs R1 vocabulary around transcript continuity, run truth, and source grounding.
The accepted P1.2 interpretation note informs the fixed chat-native wedge and projection-vs-ontology discipline that R1 must preserve.

notes: P4.3 accepted R1 contract pack defining the first public chat-native release promise, shared primitives, projection-only additions, package floors, exit criteria, and explicit refusals.
Accepted for downstream use as the R1 release-contract baseline feeding P4.4 and later release packs plus Phase 6 package planning.
